9:
"And he said, Who art thou? And she answered, I am Ruth thine handmaid: spread therefore thy skirt over thine handmaid; for thou art a near kinsman." The witnesses note Ruth's appeal that Boaz spread his skirt over her, for he was a near kinsman — the crux of the suppliant who claims the redeemer's covenant protection by right of kinship. On "spread therefore thy skirt over thine handmaid; for thou art a near kinsman," interpreters expound the appeal. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the claim of the redeemer's covenant — "and she answered, I am Ruth thine handmaid: spread therefore thy skirt over thine handmaid; for thou art a near kinsman": Ruth, owning herself his handmaid, appeals to Boaz to take her under his protection by marriage, as the near kinsman; the truth that the kinsman-redeemer is bound to redeem his own, and the suppliant may plead that bond, the right of the goel that points to the great Redeemer of his people, "thy Maker is thine husband; the LORD of hosts is his name... thy Redeemer," Isaiah 54:5; The figure of the suppliant who claims the redeemer's covenant protection by right of kinship.

13:
